content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Rachel Le Roux 
Deputy City Attorney 
1017 Middlefield Road 
Redwood City, CA 94063 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-103 
Dear Ms. Le Roux: 
June 16, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Redwood City Planning 
Commissioner Rick Hunter regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing· advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
May Commissioner Hunter participate in decisions pertaining to five downtown projects 
(described below) ranging from 1,048 to 1,989 feet away from the offices of Certified Public 
Accountant Robert Memole who employs the Commissioner? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Based on the nature of Memole's business, it is not foreseeable that the projects will 
affect the value of Memole's business. 
FACTS 
You stated that Redwood City is experiencing rapid growth in its downtown area, and 
several development projects are expected to come before the Planning Commission in the 
foreseeable future. Development in the downtown area is governed by the Downtown Precise Plan 
("DTPP"). The DTPP establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 183-
acre DTPP area. It sets Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD Caps") of2,500 net new 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-103 
Page No. 2 
dwelling units; 100,000 square feet of net new retail development; 500,000 square feet of net new 
commercial office space; and 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units 
and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the relevant 
MAD Cap. 
As of May 11, 2016, after accounting for all approved projects, the following development 
units are left: 
• 892 net new dwelling units; 
• 99,343 square feet of net new retail development; 
• 0 square feet of net new commercial office space; and 
• 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps. 
You stated that most of the projects expected to come before the Planning Commission 
involve some combination of residential and retail uses. There are enough remaining residential 
dwelling units and retail square feet of development under the relevant MAD Caps to allow for each 
of the proposed residential and retail projects. Because no commercial office space remains 
available under the MAD Cap, no new office space may be developed unless (a) new squaFe 
footage becomes available through removal of current office space elsewhere in the DTPP area or 
(b) the City Council increases the commercial office space MAD Cap through an amendment to the 
DTPP. One expected project includes new office space, and a DTPP amendment will be required 
for that project. 
Commissioner Hunter is semi-retired Certified Public Accountant ("CPA") who works as an 
hourly employee for Robert Memole, a CPA who has an office at 2317 Broadway Street, Suite 
Number 100, Redwood City CA 94063 (hereafter "Memole"). The address is located within 
Redwood City's DTPP. Memole is a ground floor office on a busy block of downtown Redwood 
City. The office has floor to ceiling windows facing out onto the street, with a circular decal sign 
approximately two feet in diameter identifying the business as a "Memole & Company, Certified 
Public Accountant" displayed in the center of the window. The vast majority of Memole's clientele 
is derived from referrals from existing clients. To the best of Mr. Memole's recollection, he has had 
three new walk-in clients over the last six years. 
Memole does not have a website. You stated that a Google search for "Certified Public 
Accountant in Downtown Redwood City" lists two full pages of results before the third page 
contains a listing for "Memole Robert CPA Redwood City, 94063" on www.manta.com, a website 
that describes itself as an online resource for small businesses. Commrssioner Hunter has worked 
for Memo le for three and a half years. He works full time during tax season, and off and on the rest 
of the year. 81 % of the work he does in a year is done during tax season, 15% he does in 
September/October when returns for those who filed extensions come due, and 4% is comprised of 
work he does sporadically the remainder of the year. Commissioner Hunter derives more than $500 
a year in income from Memole. He has no equity interest in Memole and no property interest in the 
office on Broadway Street. 


QUESTIONS AND ANALYSIS 
File No. A-16-103 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. They 
include the following: 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on tenns available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
Memole is an interest of the commissioner as his employer and a source of income. 
However, a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Note that the 
foreseeability and materiality analysis must be conducted on a decision-by-decision basis, unless 
the decisions are inextricably interrelated.2 
You have described the following decisions: 
1. Greystar Development has applied for a Planned Community Pennit and Tentative Parcel Map to 
construct an eight-story, approximately 346-unit multifamily residential development with 
approximately 6,000 feet of ground floor retail and two levels of underground parking at 1409 El 
Camino Real. Of the 346 units, 36 will be affordable housing units on-site, and Greystar will 
submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. Greystar 
proposes to provide approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile 
spaces for retail use, and 93 bicycle spaces. The Greystar site is 1,989 feet away from Memole. 
2. Habitat/or H11111a11ity, Greater San Francisco, has applied for a Planned Community Pennit to 
construct a six-story, 25,000 square foot residential building at 612 Jefferson Avenue. The 
project would consist of approximately 20 affordable housing units, and Habitat for Humanity 
will submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. The 
project may not meet the DTPP parking requirements and may require an allowance for such 
under-provision of on-site parking. The project site is 1,048 feet away from Memole. 
3. The Acclaim Co111pa11ies has applied for a Planned Community Permit and a Tentative Parcel 
Map to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of approximately 
86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of retail and 80,000 square 
feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project, which would combine several existing lots. 
Acclaim proposes to include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 parking 
2 Decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation l 8706(b ). ) 


File No. A-16-103 
Page No. 4 
stalls where 260 are required by the DTPP and proposes to pay a parking in-lieu fee for the 45 
spaces not provided on site. Acclaim also proposes to provide 56 bicycle spaces. Because the 
project would exceed the office MAD Cap under the DTPP, Acclaim is seeking an amendment to 
the General Plan and DTPP to allow for more office development. In addition, because the 
project would exceed the DTPP MAD Cap contemplated in the DTPP EIR, a new EIR will be 
prepared. The Acclaim site is 1,490 feet away from Memole. 
4. Anton Developmellf Company proposes to construct a residential building at 801 Brewster 
Avenue. The Project would consist of approximately 50 very low income affordable multi­
family units and 200 market-rate units. In connection with the project, the Planning Commission 
would be asked to approve a Planned Community Permit and an affordable housing plan. The 
site is 1,306 feet away from Memole. 
5. MidPe11 Housi11g proposes to build a mixed-use building at 707 and 777 Bradford Street. The 
site includes four existing parcels, including two parcels that were acquired in 2004 and 2006 
with fonner Redwood City Redevelopment Agency Low-Moderate Housing Set Aside funds. 
The project would include approximately 108 units of rental housing affordable for very low 
income seniors, space for childcare or a nonprofit, and a publicly accessible park. In connection 
with the project, the Planning Commission would be asked to approve a Downtown Planned 
Community Permit and an affordable housing plan. The Bradford Street Affordable Housing Site 
is 1,471 fett away from Memole. 
Foreseeability and Materiality: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on 
whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 1870 l provides: "A financial 
effect ... is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency." None of the 
Commissioner's financial interests are explicitly involved in the decisions in question. Thus, 
pursuant to Regulation 1870 I (b ), if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and 
more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be 
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected 
absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.3 provides, in relevant part, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of 
a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a source of income is material if: 
• 
The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is otherwise named or 
identified as the subject of the proceeding. 
Memole is not a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or otherwise named or 
identified as the subject of the proceeding. Moreover, materiality will be found under Regulation 
l 8702.3(a)(4) if: 
• 
The source is a business entity and a prudent person with sufficient information would find 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change 
in the value of a privately-held business entity. (See also Regulation 18702.1 (b ). ) 


File No. A-16-103 
Page No. 5 
The nature of Memole's business suggests that a prudent person would not find it 
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of any of the decisions would independently 
contribute to a change in the value the business. You stated that Memo le relies minimally, if at all, 
on walk-up business. The vast majority of sales are by referral. Moreover, the distances are great 
enough that there would appear to be minor if any anticipated traffic and related effects on the 
operation of Memo le. Thus, none of the projects would have any foreseeable financial effect on the 
value of Memole. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Assis ant General Counsel 
. Leg Division

May Commissioner Hunter participate in decisions pertaining to five downtown projects 
(described below) ranging from 1,048 to 1,989 feet away from the offices of Certified Public 
Accountant Robert Memole who employs the Commissioner?

affect the value of Memole's business.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Commissioner Hunter participate in decisions pertaining to five downtown projects 
(described below) ranging from 1,048 to 1,989 feet away from the offices of Certified Public 
Accountant Robert Memole who employs the Commissioner?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Based on the nature of Memole's business, it is not foreseeable that the projects will 
affect the value of Memole's business.
